next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Tomas counted his steps the way his first coach had taught him — *uno, dos, tres* — the rhythm locked in before his body had ever been touched by engineers, back when counting was the only technology available to a thirteen-year-old on a dirt track outside Quito. Three years of neural integration hadn't erased it. The enhancement handled stride frequency to the hundredth of a second now, smoothed his approach pattern into something that felt less like running and more like a machine discovering what running was for, but the counting stayed. His one piece of untouched equipment.

The indoor track smelled like hot rubber and recycled air. Mid-morning. A group session — four athletes spread across the facility's straight and curve. Kai was doing light sprint work on the far lane, his stride still favoring the shoulder that had healed wrong after the blackout crash five weeks ago. Two other athletes Tomas knew only by event — a pole vaulter from the Dutch program, a middle-distance runner whose name he kept forgetting and felt bad about — occupied the curve. The high ceilings swallowed the sound of their footfalls and returned it as a faint percussion, a dozen rubber impacts overlapping into something that was almost rhythm.

Tomas had called his mother last night. She'd wanted to know about the weather, so he'd described the snowmelt off the peaks — how the meltwater ran in channels beside the road down to Meiringen, how the Alps looked different now that the ice was pulling back, exposing gray rock underneath that had been hidden all winter. His sister Valeria h...
open_hooks_to_carry: Lena conscious during the overnight firmware rewrite: heat along the neural link, pressure behind her eyes, vision splitting into dual perspectives
Post-patch diagnostics: desync stabilizes but reaction times slower and sensory acuity blunted
The silence where the system's ambient processing used to be—Lena has never heard this silence before
Swimming laps that feel ordinary for the first time in her life
prev_tail_excerpt:
..."I'm in room 214 if you want to talk."

Lena nodded. Jin-seo left.

Margot left next. No announcement, no exit line. She picked up her jacket from the chair back and walked out. The feed interface on her wrist caught the hallway light, and then the door closed behind her.

The redheaded runner left. The South African runner shook Yusuf's hand and left. Yuki stayed another minute, flexing her left hand open and closed, open and closed, before she stood and went without saying anything.

Yusuf closed his laptop. He stood in the half-empty room and looked at the chairs arranged in their ragged semicircle, and for a moment his face did something unguarded — not disappointment exactly, but the particular tiredness of someone who had bet on honesty and watched it buy him nothing.

"I'm in the calibration bay tonight," he said. To the room, but he was looking at Lena. "If anyone has questions."

He left. The door closed. Through the glass, the mountains stood in the last of the light, the snowmelt still ticking off the eaves in its uneven rhythm.

"You OK?" Kai asked.

Lena didn't answer immediately. She was looking at her hands in her lap. The thumbs had stopped moving.

"Both options are a different person," she said.

Kai didn't have anything to say to that. He sat with her in the emptying room, thumb pressed into the knot in his wrist where the bone sat wrong, while the light went off the mountains and the water kept ticking off the eaves and the argument hung in the air the way arguments do when nobody wins them — not fading, just settling, becoming part of the room's weight.
secondary_character_beats: Dara watches Lena after the patch with visible confusion—she does not understand why Lena would choose diminished performance and silence over integrated optimization
state_deltas_required: The patch installation requires consciousness through a night-long sensory ordeal; the result is uncertain
The cure works but reveals what it costs—the silence where the system was is its own form of loss
